does: This paper presents controlled-release prompting, a practical attack that exploits the computational gap between lightweight prompt filters and the large language models they protect. The authors generate adversarial prompts that are computationally intractable for typical input filters to parse but remain solvable by the target LLM itself, enabling the model to execute harmful requests. They demonstrate successful attacks against four major deployed chat platforms (Google Gemini, DeepSeek Chat, xAI Grok, and Mistral Le Chat) where simpler adversarial methods fail, and show the technique can extract copyrighted training data from Gemini. The approach bridges theory and practice by instantiating the impossibility result of Ball et al. into a concrete vulnerability that works on real-world systems without requiring model modification.
connects: This work builds directly on Ball et al.'s theoretical result establishing that no filter significantly faster than the protected model can universally defend against all adversarial prompts under standard cryptographic assumptions. It connects to the broader literature on prompt injection attacks, jailbreaking techniques, and alignment bypass methods that have shown LLM safety measures are often circumventable. The paper also relates to research on the computational complexity of adversarial robustness and the resource asymmetries inherent in defensive AI systems, extending prior work on prompt filtering vulnerabilities by providing the first practical instantiation of the theoretical impossibility framework at scale across commercial systems.
enables: This research demonstrates that current deployment practices for prompt filtering are fundamentally vulnerable to resource-aware adversarial attacks, pointing toward the need for architectural changes in how LLM safety is enforced. Future work could explore defenses that move beyond fast input filters toward approaches that distribute computational cost more evenly between attack and defense, or that leverage properties of the models themselves rather than external filters. The findings open questions about whether any practical filtering approach can achieve robust alignment given the computational constraints of real systems, and suggest that effective safeguards may require deeper model-level modifications or fundamentally different security paradigms rather than lightweight input-side protections.
